coredata.dc:description: Corrosion is a global issue, costing the economy more than $2.5 trillion annually and affecting metal structures across various industries. Corrosion inhibitors effectively mitigate metal corrosion and are popular in the field of corrosion protection, due to their efficiency, low cost, and environmentally friendly properties. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, show significant potential as corrosion inhibitors. Currently, various methods have been developed to synthesize efficient CD inhibitors, and the structures of CD inhibitors can be easily regulated by changing the synthetic parameters. More importantly, CDs demonstrate promising inhibition performance in various environments, including acidic solutions, NaCl solutions, CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, microbial-corroded solutions, and alkaline solutions. Moreover, their related inhibition mechanisms are revealed through electrochemical methods, surface characterization, adsorption isotherms, and theoretical calculations. This review comprehensively summarizes the research progress of CDs as novel corrosion inhibitors, containing the synthesis methods of CD inhibitors, their applications in different solutions, and their corrosion inhibition mechanisms. This review would inspire researchers to tap into the full potential of CDs as novel corrosion inhibitors.

head.ce:title: Research progress of carbon dots as novel corrosion inhibitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Corrosion is a global issue, costing the economy more than $2.5 trillion annually and affecting metal structures across various industries. Corrosion inhibitors effectively mitigate metal corrosion and are popular in the field of corrosion protection, due to their efficiency, low cost, and environmentally friendly properties. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, show significant potential as corrosion inhibitors. Currently, various methods have been developed to synthesize efficient CD inhibitors, and the structures of CD inhibitors can be easily regulated by changing the synthetic parameters. More importantly, CDs demonstrate promising inhibition performance in various environments, including acidic solutions, NaCl solutions, CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, microbial-corroded solutions, and alkaline solutions. Moreover, their related inhibition mechanisms are revealed through electrochemical methods, surface characterization, adsorption isotherms, and theoretical calculations. This review comprehensively summarizes the research progress of CDs as novel corrosion inhibitors, containing the synthesis methods of CD inhibitors, their applications in different solutions, and their corrosion inhibition mechanisms. This review would inspire researchers to tap into the full potential of CDs as novel corrosion inhibitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion, as a significant global issue, severely impacts the integrity and longevity of metal structures. This spans various sectors, from traditional infrastructure and manufacturing to the transportation industry, and extends into novel high-tech fields such as new energy, the aerospace industry, and electronic devices [1–3]. According to the National Association of Corrosion Engineers, worldwide corrosion results in considerable economic losses, surpassing USD 2.5 trillion annually [4–6]. It also exacerbates environmental degradation, inevitably increasing greenhouse gas emissions [2,7–9]. In response, several advancements have been made, including the development of corrosion inhibitors [10–12], self-assembled films [13,14], surface spraying or passivation [15–17], and electrochemical protection techniques [18,19]. Notably, benefitting from their high efficiency, low cost, good stability, and compatibility with various metals, corrosion inhibitors are renowned [10,20]. And they can drastically reduce the corrosion rate with minimal dosage [10,11] to safeguard metal components effectively.

The current corrosion inhibitor market is comprised of inorganic and organic inhibitors [10,21,22]. Inorganic inhibitors, such as phosphate, nitrite, chromate, zinc, and mercury salts, have shown effectiveness in inhibiting metal corrosion. Nonetheless, they inevitably brought significant environmental and health concerns, including toxicity, instability, and eutrophication propensity [2,23–25]. By comparison, most organic inhibitors offer a degree of biodegradability and relatively lower toxicity. These inhibitors typically contain heteroatoms with lone pair electrons, which facilitates their adsorption onto the metal surface, thus enhancing resistance against corrosion [26–28]. Despite these benefits, organic inhibitors still suffer from poor solubility [29], tedious preparation process [30–32], and the use of hazardous solvents [32,33], limiting their broader application and acceptance. Therefore, it is urgent to develop novel corrosion inhibitors with good solubility, facile preparation methods, environmental compatibility, and excellent corrosion inhibition efficiency (η).

Exhilaratingly, carbon dots (CDs) as a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, exhibit exceptional properties including low toxicity, high specific surface area, tunable surface chemistry, notable water solubility, and small-size effect [34–39]. These features make CDs widely applied in areas such as biomedicine [40], optoelectronic devices [41], catalysis [42], chemical sensing [43], lubrication [44], and civil engineering materials [45]. More importantly, the above advantages position CDs as promising substitutes for eco-friendly, and low-toxicity corrosion inhibitors. CDs are typically characterized as carbon-based nanoparticles with inherent fluorescence and diameters under 10 nm. Generally, a single CD encompasses a carbon core and O/N-containing surface groups. According to their different structural characteristics, CDs can be divided into three categories: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and polymer dots (PDs) (Fig. 1 a) [46]. GQDs consist of single or multiple layers of graphene, with a lateral size exceeding their height. In contrast, CNDs are predominantly spherical, with lateral dimensions approximately equivalent to their height. CNDs are further classified into carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with amorphous structures and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a distinct crystalline lattice and the quantum confinement effect. PDs possessing cross-linked polymeric structures are typically synthesized through the polymerization of small molecules or polymer precursors. In recent years, CDs have emerged as potent corrosion inhibitors for inhibiting metal corrosion in diverse environments [47–50].

Currently, considerable efforts have been dedicated to exploring various methods for synthesizing CDs as effective corrosion inhibitors [51–54]. Among them, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method is the most frequently used owing to its one-pot facile characteristic, environmentally-friendly raw materials, and ease of regulating the particle size and element composition of the product. With the advancement in the synthesis methods for CD inhibitors, the sizes, heteroatom doping, and surface groups of CDs can be altered by adjusting the reaction conditions, resulting in significant corrosion inhibition effects for metals in various solutions, including acidic solutions, NaCl solutions, CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, microbial-corroded solutions, and alkaline solutions [50,55–58]. Apart from the significant advancements made in the corrosion application of CDs, the related inhibition mechanisms in various corrosive media are also revealed by detection analyses and theoretical calculations.

So far, only a handful of reviews provide insights into the synthesis methods, application scenarios, and inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors [56,59–61]. Specifically, the review conducted by Elsadek and colleagues [60] focused on the application of GQDs in corrosion inhibition. However, they primarily summarized GQDs as corrosion protection coating additives rather than inhibitors. Subsequently, Quraishi et al. [61] underscored the superiority of CDs over traditional carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerene in corrosion inhibition, and outlined the application of CDs as aqueous-phase inhibitors for copper, carbon steel, and mild steel. Nevertheless, their analyses were shallow due to fewer cited literature. Berdimurodov et al. [56] thoroughly discussed the employment and inhibition mechanisms of CDs as corrosion inhibitors for diverse metals in harsh environments. However, the review regarding the inhibition mechanisms of CDs lacked depth. More recently, Wang et al. [59] outlined the synthesis routes and modification strategies of CDs and emphasized their applications in metal corrosion and protection. Nevertheless, this review predominantly focused on CDs as smart corrosion protection coating additives, inadvertently ignoring their employment as corrosion inhibitors. As a result, existing reviews have some notable drawbacks: (1) Focusing on CDs as coating additives rather than corrosion inhibitors; (2) Lacking a comprehensive summary of synthesis methods of CDs as corrosion inhibitors; (3) Insufficiently analyzing inhibition mechanisms of CD-based inhibitors. More importantly, the development of CD-based inhibitors has accelerated, with related research significantly increasing over the past two years.

Considering the facts above, this review aims to comprehensively summarize the synthetic methods of CD inhibitors, their application scenarios, and the related inhibition mechanism (Fig. 1b). Specifically, various synthetic methods for preparing CDs as corrosion inhibitors, including hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave treatment, pyrolysis synthesis, electrochemical synthesis, and chemical oxidation method are concluded in Section 2; The performance of CD inhibitors in various corrosive environments containing acidic solutions, NaCl solutions, CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, microbiologically influenced solutions, and chloride-contaminated alkaline solutions, are outlined in Section 3; And the corrosion inhibition mechanisms of CDs as inhibitors are summarized in Section 4. Ultimately, the prospects and challenges for CDs as next-generation corrosion inhibitors are discussed in depth in Section 5. This review is conducive to tapping into the full potential of CDs as novel corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs as corrosion inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The structural diversity of CDs mainly stems from differences in the raw materials and synthetic methods. These variations subsequently impact the corrosion inhibition performance of CD-based inhibitors. Based on the type of carbon sources, the synthetic methods for CDs are categorized into two classes: top-down and bottom-up approaches [51,52,54,62]. Regarding top-down ways, large-scale carbon materials like graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanofibers are broken down into smaller CDs. While the bottom-up methods synthesize CDs through the polymerization and carbonization of small molecules or polymers. Both top-down and bottom-up methods can be achieved by similar preparation techniques such as hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave treatment, pyrolysis, electrochemical synthesis, and chemical oxidation.

Hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis involves mixing reactants (small molecules or bulk materials) with water or solvents in a sealed container, where they polymerize, carbonize, or fragment to create CDs under elevated temperature and pressure [63]. Fig. 2 illustrates a schematic flow for the preparation of CDs by hydrothermal/solvothermal methods.

Zhao's group first reported an N-doped CD (N-CD) inhibitor synthesized by heating aminosalicylic acid (ASA) at 200 °C for 18 h in ethanol using the solvothermal method. The resulting CD inhibitor exhibited uniform nanoparticles with an average size of 3 nm. More importantly, CDs were rich in O- and N-containing functional groups, significantly facilitating their corrosion inhibition performance for carbon steel [47]. Henceforth, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method provides a facile path for preparing efficient CD inhibitors with heteroatom doping. Inspired by this, Wang [68], Ye [69,70], Chen [71,72], and other researchers [73–76] hydrothermally treated N-containing raw materials, like imidazole, n-butylamine, and ethylenediamine to obtain N-CD inhibitors for different metals in different solutions. When employing both N- and S-containing raw materials in hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, researchers produced N- and S-doped CD (N, S-CD) inhibitors [49,77–80], which was conducive to improving their corrosion inhibition performance. Similarly, small molecules such as sulfamic acid [57], l-cysteine [81], cerium nitrate hexahydrate [82], and cupric chloride [50,83], were applied to introduce S, Ce, or Cu elements into CD inhibitors via hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis methods for improving the η value of CD inhibitors (Table 1 ). Compared to the two-step hydrothermal method [67,84], these one-pot hydrothermal methods are simpler and more convenient, producing CD inhibitors with similar corrosion inhibition abilities.

In addition to small molecules, He's group hydrothermally treated easy-acquired and environmentally-friendly litchi leaves to obtain N-CDs as a novel kind of inhibitor [85] without toxic chemicals (Fig. 3 a). They report the first biomass-derived CDs as a promising corrosion inhibitor, setting the groundwork for developing real green CD inhibitors. Currently, researchers have successfully prepared biomass-derived CD inhibitors from zanthoxylum bungeanum leaves (Fig. 3e) [86], grapefruit peels (Fig. 3c) [87], mango pulp (Fig. 3b) [88], melon seed shell (Fig. 3f) [89], and chicken protein (Fig. 3d) [90], etc., demonstrating comparable inhibition performance with CD inhibitors from small molecules. However, the low yield of biomass-derived CD inhibitors prepared via the hydrothermal/solvothermal method remains unsolved, requiring further investigations to improve their yield. Apart from easily doping heteroatoms into CDs, the hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis method directly regulates the particle size and element composition of CD inhibitors by controlling the heating time [89,91], temperature [67,89], and proportions of raw materials [65,92]. This lays a solid foundation for investigating the relationship between the structure and the corrosion inhibition performance of CD inhibitors. It is worth noting that specific types of CDs can be directly prepared through hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment. He's team synthesized GQDs and N-doped GQDs (NGQDs) by a one-pot hydrothermal approach [66]. By simply adjusting the raw materials, GQDs and NGQDs with comparable crystalline structures/diameters and different element compositions were prepared, beneficial to reveal the influence of N incorporation on the inhibition performance of GQDs.

In brief, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method is the most frequently used method to prepare CD inhibitors, owing to its convenience, wide source of raw materials, and easy regulation of the particle size and element composition of CDs. Nevertheless, there are some obvious shortcomings, such as high time and energy consumption, to be addressed.

Under microwave treatment, reactants undergo dehydration, condensation, and carbonization to form CDs [63,95]. This process shortens reaction time to several minutes to rapidly obtain CD inhibitors. Li's group first reported a simple microwave method to synthesize CD inhibitors, costing only 3 min to synthesize N-CDs with the size of 4‒8 nm (Fig. 4 c) [57]. This method produced CDs rapidly, thus saving reaction time. Subsequently, they continued applying the microwave method and synthesized N-CD inhibitors with good water solubility and dispersibility (Fig. 4a and b) [93,94]. Interestingly, when the heating time was 240 s, the average particle size of CD inhibitors was about 10 nm [94]; While the heating time was 270 s, their average particle size was 16 nm [93]. It is noticeable that the particle size of CD inhibitors is possibly related to microwave treating time [96]. Microwave treatment boosts preparation efficiency and saves energy; Nevertheless, it is relatively less applied compared to hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, probably attributed to its low repeatability.

The pyrolysis synthesis route carbonizes carbon sources at high temperatures to obtain CD inhibitors. By adjusting the pyrolysis temperature and time, the particle size and crystallinity of CD inhibitors are easy to regulate [69,97]. As displayed in Fig. 5 a and b, Ye's group pioneered this approach, synthesizing CD inhibitors with various size distributions (3‒6, 2‒3, and 10‒15 nm) by heating at 150, 180, and 210 °C [97]. This method produces CD inhibitors with consistent atomic compositions but different size distributions, which is crucial for studying the effect of their particle size on corrosion inhibition performance. Enlightened by this, Zhao and his colleagues produced CD inhibitors with different N types (pyridine-like N, pyrrole-like N, and graphitic N) by simply changing pyrolysis time (Fig. 5c) [98]. The aforementioned research demonstrates that the pyrolysis synthesis route can effectively regulate the structure of CD inhibitors, conducive to studying the relationship between their structure and corrosion inhibition performance. However, despite its efficiency and cost-effectiveness [99], the pyrolysis method faces challenges such as high energy consumption, tedious post-treatment, and low production rate, leading to its limited employment.

Generally, electrochemical synthesis utilizes conductive carbon materials as both working electrodes and carbon sources. In 2015, Liu first employed an electrochemical method to synthesize GQD inhibitors by exfoliating a graphite rod for 6 h, using triammonium citrate as the electrolyte. As exhibited in Fig. 6 a, the resulting GQDs had flat-sheet structures ranging from 5 to 30 nm and were rich in O-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups [100]. Fu et al. later employed a similar procedure as Liu's method, obtaining GQD inhibitors with semblable sizes and morphology (Fig. 6b) [28]. However, the η value of these GQDs was 10–20 % lower compared to CD inhibitors synthesized by other methods [47,97,98]. As a result, this approach was not used for the next few years. Six years later, Zhou et al. changed the electrolyte to NH4HCO3, successfully doping N heteroatoms into CD inhibitors [102], achieving CDs with more than 30 % N content and an average particle size of 25.65 nm. This marked the first instance of synthesizing heteroatom-doped CDs, rather than GQDs, with relatively large particle sizes using the electrochemical method. Different from previous studies, He et al. prepared CD inhibitors by altering the carbon source into anhydrous ethanol (Fig. 6c) [101], producing CDs with particle size distribution ranging from 5 to 10 nm. Interestingly, the CD inhibitors prepared via this method exhibited the most uniform size distribution, contributing to superior corrosion inhibition properties. Overall, the electrochemical synthesis method primarily yields GQDs rich in O-containing functional groups, but their relatively low corrosion inhibition performance likely limits its broader application.

The chemical oxidation method is simple and scalable, employing oxidants such as HNO3, and their mixture with H2SO4, or O2 to oxidize carbon sources, thus producing CD inhibitors. For example, Yang and his group prepared the CD inhibitor under the O2 atmosphere for 24 h (Fig. 7 a) [103]. This endowed the CD inhibitor with numerous O- and N-containing functional groups, enhancing its corrosion inhibition performance. However, the chemical oxidation method is time-consuming, and the use of strong acid is not aligned with green synthesis principles, limiting its broader application.

Synthesizing CD inhibitors at ambient temperatures is attractive due to its energy efficiency. To fulfill this, Zhu et al. fabricated CD inhibitors by stirring NaOH with acetaldehyde for 3 d at room temperature (Fig. 7b) [104]. This method enables the simple, ambient-temperature preparation of CD inhibitors, but still suffers from time consumption. Subsequently, based on Schiff base reactions, Li et al. used p-benzoquinone and o-phenylenediamine as precursors to prepare CD inhibitors at room temperature for 2 h (Fig. 7d) [106]. This approach is not only energy-efficient but also time-saving, and the produced CD inhibitors with Schiff base structures show favorable corrosion inhibition performance. To further shorten reaction time, He et al. developed an in-situ, facile, low-cost, and universal approach to rapidly prepare CD inhibitors in the pickling solution. In detail, low-cost and easily available saccharide (glucose, soluble starch, fructose, or saccharose) was adopted as a precursor; And concentrated H2SO4 acted as both a dehydrator for preparing CD inhibitors and an acid source for pickling solution. The pickling solution with CD inhibitors was obtained within the shortest time of 3 min at room temperature (Fig. 7c) [105]. This is the fastest preparation method of CD-based pickling solution reported to date. More importantly, this reported method not only allows for room-temperature synthesis but also avoids the tedious post-treatment process, thereby making it faster and simpler than other approaches.

All in all, considerable efforts have been made to explore the synthesis of CD inhibitors. The size, heteroatom doping, and surface groups of CD inhibitors can be adjusted by altering the preparation conditions. Obviously, each method has its advantages and limitations. Microwave treatment is rapid and energy-efficient but lacks repeatability. Pyrolysis is straightforward and cost-effective but requires high energy and low production rate. Electrochemical synthesis is environmentally friendly but time-consuming and yields CD inhibitors with lower corrosion inhibition performance. Chemical oxidation endows CDs with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups but generally uses harmful oxidants. Among various preparation methods, although the hydrothermal/solvothermal approach is subject to complicated post-treatment, is considered the most ideal way owing to its advantages of precise structural control of CDs, high repeatability, high yield, and simple operation. To eliminate the need for tedious post-treatment, a promising in-situ synthetic method has been developed to rapidly and efficiently produce CD inhibitors [105]. However, this method is restricted to the preparation of CD inhibitors suitable for pickling solutions. Therefore, there is still an urgent need to develop more in-situ, scalable, efficient, eco-friendly, and controllable methods for producing CD inhibitors suitable for various environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs as corrosion inhibitors in different solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring various application scenarios of CD inhibitors has continuously been an attractive theme in recent years. In 2015, Zhou and colleagues [100] discovered that CDs function as effective corrosion inhibitors in acidic solutions. Soon after, Zhao [84] and Chen [49] extended the application scenario of CD inhibitors to NaCl solutions and CO2-saturated NaCl solutions. Additionally, CD inhibitors in microbiologically influenced corrosion [50,82] and alkaline environments [107] were reported. As a result, CD inhibitors possess broad applicability. This section discusses and analyzes the applications of CD inhibitors in these various corrosive solutions.

Acid-induced corrosion is widely found in industrial processes such as descaling, pickling, and oil well acidification [55,108,109]. To mitigate this issue, CDs as corrosion inhibitors are applied to protect metals and alloys in acidic environments. In 2015, GQDs, a type of CDs, were first reported as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, achieving an η value of 71.7 % at a concentration of 300 mg/L, as confirmed by potential dynamic polarization (PDP) measurements [100]. Subsequently, Zhao's group discovered that N-CD inhibitors had a significantly higher η value of 90.9 % for carbon steel in the same acidic solution, even at a much lower concentration of 10 mg/L (confirmed by PDP) [47]. This finding highlights that N heteroatom doping can enhance the performance of CD inhibitors. Inspired by these results, numerous researchers synthesized highly efficient N-CD inhibitors [64,68,69,110], with some achieving nearly 100 % η value for carbon steel (Fig. 8 d) [85,90,105]. Additionally, these N-CD inhibitors exhibited exceptional corrosion inhibition across various metals not only for carbon steel but also for other metals, including copper [68,82,111] and aluminum [77,112]. More importantly, they exhibit excellent long-term corrosion resistance [64,66,68,69,80]. He's team further confirmed the enhanced performance of N-doped GQDs through a combination of experimental results and theoretical calculations. As demonstrated in Fig. 8b, they found that while GQDs achieved an η value of 83.32 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L, NGQDs achieved a higher η value of 89.25 % [66]. However, despite the improvement brought by N doping, the η value did not exceed 90 % at this concentration, suggesting that GQDs may not be the ideal corrosion inhibitor. Nevertheless, CDs remain a promising candidate for the next generation of novel, effective corrosion inhibitors. Furthermore, even with N doping, the corrosion inhibition performance of CD inhibitors can vary significantly depending on the position and type of heteroatoms. Wang and his colleagues produced CD inhibitors with different N positions—amino-N, pyrrolic-N, and quaternary-N—by adjusting the heating temperatures while using the same precursors [67]. They found that CDs with the highest pyrrolic-N content exhibited the best η value in 1 M HCl, underscoring that pyrrolic-N incorporation may be more beneficial than other forms of N incorporation for improving the inhibition efficiency of CD inhibitors. This finding provides valuable guidance for synthesizing more efficient N-CD inhibitors in the future.

In addition to N doping, S doping has also demonstrated excellent corrosion inhibition performance. Yadav's group synthesized N-CD inhibitors and N, S-CD inhibitors for mild steel, achieving η values of 90 % and 96.4 %, respectively, at 100 ppm concentration in a 15 % HCl solution (Fig. 8c) [78]. Subsequently, Ren et al. prepared N-CDs and N, S-CDs for inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel, reaching η values of 87.9 % (N-CDs) and 96.4 % (N, S-CDs) at a concentration of 200 ppm (Fig. 8a) [80]. Ye's team synthesized N, S-CD inhibitors with varied precursor ratios of ammonium citrate to methionine (100:0, 95:5, 90:10, and 85:15). They found that CD inhibitors prepared with a 90:10 ratio exhibited the best η value of 98 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L in 1 M HCl (Fig. 8e) [65], indicating that while S doping enhances inhibition, more content means not better. Although S doping did enhance the corrosion inhibition efficiency, the improvement was marginal and came with an increased cost. Moreover, S is known to be toxic to the environment. Therefore, N doping alone is sufficient to achieve the desired corrosion inhibition performance of CD inhibitors.

In addition to N, S heteroatom doping, the incorporation of metal ions or atoms such as Ce [82], Cu [83], and Na [113] enhances the corrosion protection behavior of CD inhibitors. For example, Nair's group employed N-CDs and Cu, N-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs) as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in 15 % HCl. With the addition of 25 ppm Cu, N-CDs, the η value increased from 94.9 % to 96.5 %, compared to N-CD inhibitors [83]. However, this improvement in efficiency is relatively modest. Additionally, the higher cost of raw materials associated with metal doping increases the overall preparation cost, thereby reducing economic feasibility. Furthermore, since Ce already possesses inherent corrosion inhibition properties [114,115], its use may offer limited additional benefits. Moreover, the incorporation of metal elements can raise environmental concerns due to potential pollution. Therefore, N doping alone is recommended as a more effective and environmentally sustainable approach to enhancing the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CD inhibitors.

In the aforementioned literature, employing small molecules as precursors for preparing heteroatom-doped CD inhibitors can pose environmental risks. Consequently, some studies explored the use of environmentally friendly, easy-acquired biomass as precursors to synthesize heteroatom-doped CD inhibitors in acid solutions [85–88,90,116]. For instance, He and his team utilized litchi leaves to prepare N-CD inhibitors rich in O- and N-containing functional groups, achieving an impressive η value of 98.06 % for carbon steel at a concentration of 200 mg/L in 1 M HCl [85]. This efficiency is comparable to that of CD inhibitors derived from small molecule precursors, pioneered by utilizing biomass to synthesize environmentally friendly, efficient, heteroatom-doped CD inhibitors. Later on, Feng's group employed grapefruit peels to synthesize biomass-derived CD inhibitors, achieving an η value of 93.5 % at merely 25 mg/L additive [87]. This further enhances the corrosion inhibition performance of biomass-derived N-CDs. Building on these findings, researchers applied various other S-containing biomass sources, including mango pulp [88], melon seed shell [89], chicken protein [90], and pitaya peels [117], to prepare N, S-CD inhibitors, with a maximum η value of 99.66 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L, further improving inhibition efficiency. However, it is important to note that while the precursors used in these studies are derived from biomass, they are often valuable materials and may not represent the most efficient use of waste. As a result, these approaches may fall short of producing truly green and efficient CD inhibitors.

Apart from heteroatom doping, regulating the particle size of CD inhibitors also contributes to η value. Chen's group synthesized N-CD inhibitors of three different sizes at various preparation temperatures, all achieving η values exceeding 90 % for Q235 steel in 1 M HCl, with the smallest particle showing the highest efficiency [97]. This study elucidates the potential correlation between particle size and the η value of CD inhibitors. While some research demonstrated a similar trend with Chen's findings [67], others reported the opposite results [91]. In detail, the largest CD inhibitors, rich in heteroatom and surface functional groups, exhibited the highest inhibition efficiency; conversely, the smallest CD inhibitors demonstrated the opposite characteristics [91]. These studies suggest that the relationship between particle size and η value lacks a widely accepted explanation, while heteroatom doping for CD inhibitors probably has a more significant impact than particle size.

Overall, CDs show significant potential as effective corrosion inhibitors for various metals in acid environments. Extensive literature demonstrates that heteroatom doping, including N, S, and metals, can markedly enhance the performance of CD inhibitors, with η values approaching nearly 100 % [85,89,90,105]. Among these elements, N doping presents distinct advantages, warranting further investigation. Current studies highlight the differences in corrosion inhibition performance among various N doping types, as well as the impact of particle size. However, given the conflicting conclusions in existing research, it remains necessary to systematically investigate the individual effects of particle size and heteroatom doping on the performance of CD inhibitors.

In chloride-containing environments, aggressive chloride ions adsorb onto metal surfaces, and subsequently initiate pitting corrosion in metallic materials, frequently occurring in groundwater, seawater, and aqueous crude oil [56,111]. CDs as effective corrosion inhibitors provide a promising solution for metal protection from chloride ions invasion.

In 2019, Zhao's team first demonstrated the effective inhibiting abilities of CDs for carbon steel in NaCl solution [84]. They employed imidazole ionic liquid-modified CDs (IM-CDs), achieving an η value of 85.7 % in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at a concentration of 200 mg/L (Fig. 9 a). This finding highlights the effectiveness of CD inhibitors not only in acidic environments but also in NaCl environments. However, the use of costly ionic liquids [85,118] significantly increased the production cost of CD inhibitors. Moreover, the two-step preparation process of IM-CDs was both time-consuming and energy-intensive, hindering their broader application. To address these issues, Ye's group developed low-cost N-CD inhibitors using a one-step hydrothermal method, increasing the inhibition efficiency in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to 88.96 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L for steel (Fig. 9b) [48]. This approach not only reduces the cost of raw materials but also shortens the preparation time. Since then, an increasing number of researchers have synthesized highly effective CD inhibitors for NaCl solutions [48,119,120]. However, these CD inhibitors still suffer from relatively low efficiency, with η values remaining below 95 % even at concentrations of 200 mg/L. To improve the η values of CD inhibitors in NaCl solutions, Zhang's group synthesized an N-CD inhibitor with an increased N content of 23.95 %, significantly boosting the η value to 96.1 % for copper at a concentration of 200 mg/L [121]. This high efficiency is attributed to the unique structure of CD inhibitors, including various N-containing heteroatom doping and surface functional groups. Inspired by this, Yang's team synthesized N, S-CD inhibitor for Q235 steel, achieving an impressive η value of 96.5 % in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with an addition of 250 mg/L (Fig. 9c) [103]. However, the relatively modest improvement of N, S-CD inhibitor in η value compared to N-CD inhibitor suggests that N doping alone is highly effective in enhancing inhibition efficiency. While functioning as corrosion inhibitors, CDs can also serve as fluorescent probes. Wang's group developed a dual-functional CD inhibitor that combines corrosion protection with detection capabilities. As a corrosion inhibitor, CDs achieved an η value of 94.01 % for copper in a 3.5 % NaCl solution. Additionally, as fluorescent probes, CDs demonstrated a detection limit for Cr(VI) ions as low as 24 nM (Fig. 9d) [119]. This work lays the foundation for developing novel dual-functional CD inhibitors capable of self-monitoring corrosion. However, compared to the nearly 100 % η value of CD inhibitors for various metals in acidic solutions, the η value of CD inhibitors in NaCl solutions for carbon steel and copper still requires improvement. Moreover, the corrosion inhibition performance of CD inhibitors on other metals, such as aluminum, remains to be explored.

CO2-saturated NaCl solutions pose a significant corrosion threat to metal structures, particularly in oil production and transportation [56]. During oil and gas extraction, CO2 flooding is often employed to enhance production; However, CO2 tends to dissolve in the oilfield water, where the presence of Cl– and oxygen further accelerates metal corrosion [49,57]. Compared to pure NaCl solutions, the dissolution of CO2 in the water phase creates a more acidic medium. Consequently, it is necessary to employ efficient CD inhibitors for protecting metals in CO2-saturated NaCl solutions. As shown in Fig. 10 b and c, Li's team employed N-CD inhibitors for N80 steel in a CO2-saturated 3 % NaCl solution, achieving η value of 83.7 % [57] and 83.5 % [94] at a concentration of 600 mg/L, respectively. However, the maximum η value did not exceed 85 %. To address this limitation, Chen's group synthesized N, S-CD inhibitors in CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, significantly improving the η value to 93 % at a dosage of just 50 mg/L (Fig. 10a) [49]. This approach provides a valuable strategy for enhancing the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CD inhibitors in CO2-saturated NaCl solution. To further validate the corrosion resistance of CD inhibitors at elevated temperatures, Li's team prepared N, S-CD inhibitors and tested their η value under 70 °C, achieving an inhibition efficiency of 94.6 % at a concentration of 200 mg/L (Fig. 10d) [93]. This development paves the way for the preparation of high-efficiency, temperature-resistant CD inhibitors, offering a foundation for future research.

Metals used in marine environments like offshore platforms, bridges, and pipelines are particularly susceptible to seawater corrosion, facing challenges from microbiologically influenced environments and chloride-contaminated alkaline environments [50,56,122]. In recent years, CD inhibitors have been increasingly discovered for their antimicrobial corrosion and alkaline corrosion.

Rasekh's group first employed CDs in mitigating bacteria-caused corrosion. In the presence of CDs, both bacteria reproduction was effectively inhibited, thus reducing corrosion products (Fig. 11 a) [50]. Yang's work demonstrated the excellent antibacterial properties of CD inhibitors, achieving a 99.35 % inhibition efficiency at 250 mg/L (Fig. 11b) [82]. In civil engineering, reinforced concrete structures in coastal environments are prone to chloride-induced corrosion, leading to steel expansion, cracking, and deterioration (Fig. 11c). This significantly reduces their service life and causes substantial economic losses [123]. He and his colleagues explored CD inhibitors in a chloride-contaminated alkaline environment, achieving a long-term inhibition efficiency of 99 % at 400 mg/L [107]. This is the first and currently, the only study of CD inhibitors in alkaline corrosion environments, indicating vast potential for future research. Current research is limited, and future studies are expected to explore different element-doped and various-sized CD inhibitors to enhance their corrosion inhibition performance.

Based on the content of this section, CDs are effective corrosion inhibitors in various environments, including acidic solutions, NaCl solutions, CO2-saturated NaCl solutions, microbial-corroded solutions, and chloride-contaminated alkaline solutions. The inhibition performance of CD inhibitors from studies is summarized in Table 1. The inhibition efficiency of CD inhibitors is generally higher in acidic mediums, reaching over 90 %, compared to NaCl solutions [48,49,84]. That is possibly due to the different corrosion mechanisms: hydrogen evolution corrosion occurs in acidic environments, while oxygen reduction corrosion predominates in neutral environments. Moreover, CD inhibitors possess oxygen-absorbing characteristics [119], which might counteract the inhibition performance, thus decreasing the inhibition efficiency in neutral solutions. For practical engineering applications in the future, the corrosion inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors still require discussion.

Over the past decades, CD inhibitors have been effective in various corrosive solutions. However, their mechanisms require further elucidation. This section presents the primary methods for analyzing corrosion inhibition mechanisms, including electrochemical methods, surface characterization, adsorption isotherms, and theoretical calculations. By combining these methods, the corrosion inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors are analyzed in depth.

PDP measurements are employed to monitor the corrosion process and mechanisms of anodic and cathodic reactions with the protection of CD inhibitors. Specifically, the relevant parameters, including corrosion potential (E corr), cathode slope (β c), and anode slope (β a) help to elucidate their inhibition mechanism. Typically, both β c and β a shift towards lower values with the addition of CD inhibitors, which indicates that CD inhibitors suppress both cathodic and anodic reactions. Moreover, when the shift in E corr is within 85 mV, the CD inhibitor is classified as a mixed-type inhibitor. Indeed, most CD inhibitors are mixed-type corrosion inhibitors [28,66,100], while some function as either cathodic or anodic corrosion inhibitors exclusively [68,86,87].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements and their equivalent electrical circuits can confirm the adsorption of CD inhibitors on metal surfaces. Cui et al. demonstrate the addition of N-CD inhibitors exhibited a large capacitive loop in the Nyquist plots and caused the inductive loop in the equivalent circuit to disappear [47,64]. This suggests CD inhibitors form a protective layer on the steel surface, suppressing carbon steel from dissolution by blocking corrosive agents. Similarly, Ye et al. observed the loop related to CDs’ adsorption film in the Nyquist and Bode plots with the addition of N-CD inhibitors [71]. As the CD inhibitor concentration increased, the film resistance increased. This suggests that the corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDs involves the formation of a protective barrier, which prevents corrosive agents from reaching the metal surface. Similar phenomena have been observed in subsequent studies [48,67,68,119]. Nevertheless, the adsorption of CD inhibitors is not always evident in Nyquist and Bode plots [87,97,104,125,126], thereby not clearly illustrating their impact on the charge transfer process. Therefore, to gain a deeper understanding of the inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors, more advanced analytical techniques are required.

Surface characterization techniques help evaluate the surface thickness under the protection of CD inhibitors and assess the interaction between CD inhibitors and metal surfaces. Commonly used surface analysis methods include Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)/Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS), 3D Profilometry, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR).

SEM characterizes corrosion morphology, while EDS analyzes elemental composition including both corrosion products and adsorbed CD inhibitors. Ye et al. found that Cl and O elements of corrosion products decreased significantly after the addition of N-CDs [48,71,97], indicating CD inhibitors suppress corrosive Cl‒ from absorbing on metal surfaces, thereby hindering the formation of O-containing corrosion products. In Zhao's study, the N content on the steel surface increased in the presence of the N-CD inhibitor [84], indicating that CD inhibitor molecules adsorbed onto the steel surface during the inhibition process.

XPS analyzes the composition and chemical states of metal surfaces, including adsorbed CD inhibitors. In certain cases, results from XPS align with those from EDS. Zhao's group used XPS to analyze the carbon steel surface protected by CD inhibitors and found that the oxygen content decreased while the iron content gradually increased [84], indicating CD inhibitors effectively mitigate corrosion on carbon steel. This trend was also confirmed by their EDS results. However, differences are observed in some studies [48,70]. For example, when CDs and IL-CDs were added to the solution, XPS confirmed the presence of a new N element on the metal surface. In contrast, EDS results revealed an opposite trend, with N detected only in the group without CDs [127]. This discrepancy may be attributed to the limited spatial resolution of EDS [128], whereas XPS, as a high-sensitivity surface analysis technique, provides more detailed information [129]. Notably, XPS can detect N-Fe bonds (Fig. 12 a), confirming the existence of chemical bonds between CD inhibitors and metal surfaces [91,127,130,131].

Raman spectroscopy can detect various types of corrosion products, including Fe2O3, Fe3O4, α-FeOOH, β-FeOOH, and γ-FeOOH [70,71]. Under the protection of CD inhibitors, the intensity of these peaks significantly decreased, indicating that CD inhibitors effectively inhibited the formation of corrosion products. These results are further supported by XPS analysis in some studies.

FTIR spectra can identify the interaction between CD inhibitors and metal surfaces by analyzing the presence or disappearance of specific functional groups. Zhang et al. observed that certain functional groups disappeared after CD inhibitors were absorbed onto the metal surface, indicating the formation of chemical chelating bonds (Fig. 12b and c) [76]. Similarly, the emergence of specific functional groups was found to indicate the formation of protective films [132].

3D profilometry measures the surface roughness (S a) of corroded metals to assess the effectiveness of CD inhibitors. The value of S a can be measured using various techniques, including 3D profilometers (Fig. 12d and e) [66,85,107,133,134], Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) [28,68,73,74,135], and Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope (LSCM) [70,71,98]. Typically, after the addition of CDs, the bulges on steel surfaces disappear, and the value of S a decreases, strongly confirming the mitigation of metal corrosion by CD inhibitors, which is generally corroborated by SEM results.

In addition to the methods mentioned above, contact angle measurements and XRD are sometimes used to validate the formation of protective layers of CD inhibitor molecules on steel surfaces. Yadav et al. demonstrated that adding N, S-CD inhibitors decreased wettability and increased the hydrophobic contact angle, indicating the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface [135]. However, since most CD inhibitors contain O- and N-rich functional groups that enhance water solubility, raising questions about the reliability of contact angle measurements. XRD test requires grinding surface corrosion products into powder [50], a time-consuming and labor-intensive process that often damages the corrosion products, making it less suitable compared to the in-situ methods mentioned above.

In summary, surface characterization techniques are essential for understanding the inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors. SEM and EDS provide detailed imaging and elemental analysis, demonstrating that CDs adsorb onto metal surfaces and reduce corrosion by blocking corrosive ions. XPS, with its high sensitivity, detects chemical states and bonds, such as N–Fe, revealing the formation of protective layers and chemical interactions between CD inhibitors and the metal surface. FTIR spectroscopy identifies changes in functional groups, while Raman spectroscopy identifies corrosion products, both showing how CD inhibitors form protective barriers that inhibit corrosive reactions. Additionally, 3D profilometry quantifies changes in surface roughness, assessing the extent of corrosion inhibition by CDs adsorption. Each technique provides unique perspectives on the adsorption and protective mechanisms of CDs, which are further supported by adsorption isotherm analyses discussed in the following section.

Adsorption isotherm analyses help understand how CD inhibitors adsorbed on metal surfaces [68,69,84,98]. Generally, the adsorption of inhibitors can be divided into three types—Langmuir, Temkin, and Frumkin isotherms [79]. Among these, the Langmuir isotherm model is the most frequently applied, described by the following equation [74,80]:

Here, R is the molar gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and T denotes the absolute temperature (298 K). Generally, a ΔG 0 ads value of −20 kJ/mol or less negative indicates physical adsorption, while a value more negative than −40 kJ/mol suggests chemical adsorption [28,70,73]. Both PDP and EIS results can be used to fit the isotherm model. Typically, the ΔG 0 adsvalues for CD inhibitors range between −20 kJ/mol and −40 kJ/mol, indicating the presence of physical adsorption dominated by van der Waals and electrostatic forces, and chemical adsorption involving covalent bonds due to the charge transfer process [92]. However, this method of distinguishing adsorption types has been challenged in recent literature by Kokalj [136]. He argued that the standard free energy is not a reliable criterion for differentiating between physisorption and chemisorption. Kokalj emphasized that researchers should avoid relying solely on standard adsorption-free energy, particularly when the values are moderate. Instead, more reliable criteria, such as spectroscopy for experimental analysis and molecule-surface distance and electronic structure analysis for computational modeling, should be used.

In summary, while adsorption isotherm analyses remain useful, their reliability is still a subject of debate. However, when combined with other techniques, they offer a more comprehensive understanding of the adsorption mechanisms of CD inhibitors on metal surfaces.

Theoretical simulations, primarily involving Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations, help elucidate the inhibition mechanisms of CD inhibitors at the molecular level, particularly the interactions between CDs and metal surfaces.

DFT offers insights into the electronic structure of CD inhibitors, enabling researchers to comprehend interactions between CDs and metal surfaces at the atomic level. Generally, a lower ΔE HOMO-LUMO value correlates with higher adsorption and inhibition performance. For example, Fu and his group used DFT calculations to study graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) inhibitors, which exhibited a low ΔE HOMO-LUMO value of 4.7786 eV [28], indicating that GOQDs tend to adsorb onto metal surfaces. Similarly, Ye's group demonstrated that N, S-CDs have a low energy gap (ΔE), representing strong interactions with Cu substrates [92]. Dong's team further showed that the size, shape, and functional groups of CD inhibitors significantly impact their corrosion inhibition performance through DFT calculations [137]. Recently, He's group [138] found that the ΔE value of CD aggregates (CDAs) (0.262 eV) was lower than that of CDs (1.125 eV) employing DFT calculations. This indicated that CDAs had a stronger bonding ability to the metal surface (Fig. 13 ), corresponding to better corrosion inhibition of CDAs.

MD simulations, on the other hand, indicate the interaction between the CD inhibitor molecule and the surface. Ye's group applied MD simulations to confirm that N–Cu bonds facilitate chemical adsorption on Cu surfaces [82]. Their findings showed that N-CDs have a low ΔE (0.0680 eV) and strong binding energy (E binding, 190.85 kJ⋅mol−1), indicating strong chemisorption on metal substrates [70]. Combining MD results with DFT, He's team demonstrated that OSiNDs, a type of CD, form strong bonds with metal surfaces, impeding water molecule diffusion and isolating them from the metal matrix, thereby mitigating corrosion (Fig. 14 ) [134]. In 2024, Tang et al. [138] employed MD simulations to further illustrate the interaction between CDAs/CDs and the metal surface. Concretely, The E binding values of CDAs and CDs were 381.25 and 222.08 kJ⋅mol−1, respectively. The higher E binding value of CDAs manifested stronger interaction with the metal surface, contributing to better adsorption on the metal surface.

CDs are promising corrosion inhibitors, as demonstrated by their ability to form protective barriers on metal surfaces through various mechanisms. The corrosion inhibition mechanism of CD inhibitors is summarized through electrochemical methods, surface characterization, adsorption isotherm analyses, and theoretical calculations. Due to their abundant O- and N-containing functional groups, CD inhibitors uniformly adsorb onto metal surfaces. This adsorption occurs through both chemical and physical interactions: chemical adsorption involves coordination bonds between the lone pair electrons on the heteroatoms of CD inhibitor molecules and the vacant 3d orbitals of metal atoms, while physical adsorption involves electrostatic interactions between CD inhibitors and the metal surface. Consequently, CD inhibitors effectively block aggressive ions from adsorbing onto metal surfaces, significantly reducing the formation of corrosion products. As the CD inhibitor concentration increases, the corrosion rate at the anodic and cathodic sites decreases, leading to a reduction in polarization current density.

In conclusion, this review provides an overview of the latest advancements in CD inhibitors, covering synthesis methods, applications, and inhibition mechanisms. Specifically, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method remains the most widely used due to its convenience and ability to control particle size and composition. CD inhibitors have shown broad applications in various environments, including acidic, NaCl, CO2-saturated NaCl, microbial-corroded, and alkaline solutions. Notably, CD inhibitors exhibit higher efficiency in acidic media. The inhibition mechanism of CD inhibitors is generally ascribed to their adsorption onto metal surfaces, enhancing protection against corrosive ions. Despite significant progress, several challenges remain:
(1)
Lack of a precise synthesis method for CD inhibitors. A major hurdle is the absence of an optimal technique to produce CD inhibitors with desired structures. Specifically, controlling size, shape, heteroatom doping, and functional groups remains challenging. Additionally, regardless of the method used, the resulting CDs often contain impurities like small molecules, polymers, oligomers, amorphous carbon, or carbon particles, leading to poor purity.
(2)
No standardized experimental procedure exists. The duration of experiments can affect the protective effect of CD inhibitors. The reported paper has found that their performance improves over time [84]. However, researchers report η values under varying test durations, making it hard to compare results across studies.
(3)
Excessive trial-and-error experiments in CD inhibitor development are inefficient and environmentally costly, hindering progress. Relying solely on this method can be ineffective. Machine learning (ML) shows great potential for predicting and screening CD inhibitors. The researchers have pioneered an ML approach to accurately predict CD corrosion inhibition properties, reducing time and costs [139]. As research grows, large-scale ML use is expected for precise predictions.
(4)
Incomplete understanding of inhibition mechanisms. While many studies suggest CD inhibitors form an adsorption film on metal surfaces, direct evidence is lacking. More seriously, unclear CD structures further complicate this issue. Thus, advanced techniques such as focused ion beam (FIB) are needed to better understand the interaction between CDs and metal surfaces. In addition, theoretical studies and computer simulation techniques can provide important insights into the structural determination of CDs.
